title: Needle felted hedgehog
subtitle: A compact British hedgehog in spiky brown roving with a white belly and black bead eyes
excerpt: Needle felt a hedgehog about 8 cm long from two colours of merino: a warm chestnut brown for the spiny back and a pale cream for the smooth belly. The spiky texture on the back is built by needle-felting short wisps of roving upright to suggest individual spines, then trimming with scissors to even length.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted hedgehog miniatures are a well-documented beginner project in UK craft instruction. Method draws on spike-texture technique common in guild and workshop instruction.

attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. When working the spike texture, keep fingers clear of the needle path.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: The hedgehog body is a smooth oval with the belly slightly flatter than the back. The back surface uses
to suggest the spines. The belly stays smooth cream merino. The snout tapers slightly forward from the main body oval.

items.name: Chestnut brown merino roving (body and spines)
Cream merino roving (belly and snout underside)
Small black seed beads or glass bead eyes (optional)

content.text: Roll 10 g of brown merino into a compact oval about 8 x 5 cm, slightly wider and higher at the back end than the front. Needle-felt to a firm, dense core with the size-36 needle. The belly side should be flatter; needle-felt it more on that face. The snout end tapers: add a small extra triangle of brown roving at the front and needle-felt it to a gentle point.

Lay a thin wisp of cream roving across the flat underside of the body and needle-felt it in with the size-40 needle, covering about two-thirds of the belly area from front to back. The boundary between cream and brown on the sides should be a soft blend, not a hard line.

Pull small wisps of brown roving about 2 cm long. Fold each wisp in half and hold the fold against the back surface of the body. Needle-felt the fold into the body with three or four rapid stabs. Release: the two free ends stand up as a small spike. Repeat over the entire back surface, working from the rump forward, spacing the spikes about 5 mm apart. When the back is covered, use small scissors to trim all spikes to an even 8-10 mm length.

Needle-felt a tiny ball of black roving to the snout tip for the nose. Attach the eye beads by threading them onto a strong length of thread, passing through the head at the eye position, and knotting tightly. If not using beads, needle-felt two tiny dark spots of black merino as described in the needle-felt-bee and needle-felt-rabbit entries.

items.fix: Use three or four firmer stabs at the fold rather than a single stab. The fold should disappear below the surface; only the two free ends should be visible.
items.cause: The fold was not felted deeply enough into the body core
items.problem: The spines fall out when the hedgehog is handled

items.fix: Work in rows from rump to snout, filling each row before moving forward. Gaps can be filled after the main coverage by adding extra spikes.
items.cause: Spikes not spaced evenly, or some areas skipped
items.problem: The spine texture looks patchy with gaps
